title: External call to LEVERAGE_MANAGER can silently succeed when the address has no code
description:
Root cause & mechanism
Multiple parts of the RelativePositionManager system perform high-level external calls to addresses that are not verified to contain contract runtime code and/or to be the expected contract type. In the EVM, calling an address with no code (EOA, not-yet-deployed predeploy, or a self-destructed contract) can return success with empty returndata. When the Solidity call is to a function that returns no value (or when the caller does not decode/validate returndata), the call may not revert, so the caller can proceed (and even emit events) despite the callee having executed no logic. Similarly, calling a “wrong” contract that has a permissive fallback can also return success without performing the intended operation.

This creates “silent success” / “no-op” scenarios that can brick or desynchronize the position lifecycle and any off-chain/accounting logic that assumes the operation happened because the transaction succeeded and events were emitted.

Trigger conditions / assumptions
- A target address used for an external call is an EOA or otherwise has no runtime code (e.g., destroyed contract), or is not the expected contract (e.g., a non-PositionAccount contract with a permissive fallback).
- Specific triggers in this codebase include:
  1) LEVERAGE_MANAGER is configured to an address with no code; PositionAccount forwards enterLeverage to it.
  2) RelativePositionManager.executePositionAccountCall is invoked with a positionAccount address that is not a deployed PositionAccount tied to this manager (EOA, wrong contract, or contract with permissive fallback).
  3) RelativePositionManager.setPositionAccountImplementation is called with an implementation address that is non-zero but has no code; the value is then permanently locked, and clones can still be created pointing to a non-contract implementation.

Impact
- Silent no-op execution: functions can “succeed” on-chain while performing no leverage operation and/or no intended generic calls.
- Unexpected state and operational desynchronization: downstream logic (manager/UI/off-chain monitoring) may assume a position was opened/scaled or an emergency action executed because the transaction succeeded and relevant events were emitted (e.g., EnterLeverageForwarded), when nothing actually happened.
- Broken emergency procedures: executePositionAccountCall may report success while not rescuing/moving funds as expected.
- Bricked/incorrect position lifecycle due to misconfiguration: if POSITION_ACCOUNT_IMPLEMENTATION is set to an EOA/no-code address, ClonesUpgradeable.cloneDeterministic can still deploy minimal proxies referencing that implementation, and subsequent IPositionAccount interactions may not reliably revert (delegatecall to no-code can succeed with empty returndata). Because isPositionAccountImplementationLocked is set to true in the same transaction, this misconfiguration becomes effectively irreversible without upgrading the manager.

Exploitation / reproduction notes (PoC highlights)
- Configure LEVERAGE_MANAGER to an address with no runtime code, then call PositionAccount.enterLeverage(...). The call to ILeverageStrategiesManager(LEVERAGE_MANAGER).enterLeverage(...) can return success with empty returndata; enterLeverage will emit EnterLeverageForwarded and complete, despite no leverage strategy being executed.
- Call RelativePositionManager.executePositionAccountCall with positionAccount set to an EOA (or a contract with a permissive fallback). The high-level call IPositionAccount(positionAccount).genericCalls(targets, data) can return success without executing the intended logic, causing the manager-side operation to succeed silently.
- Call setPositionAccountImplementation with a non-zero EOA/no-code address. The contract locks the implementation (isPositionAccountImplementationLocked = true) and emits PositionAccountImplementationSet. Later clones created via ClonesUpgradeable.cloneDeterministic will point to the no-code implementation, leading to silent no-op / bricked behavior on subsequent interactions.

Code-location anchors (if present in the inputs)
- contracts/RelativePositionManager/PositionAccount.sol
  - function enterLeverage(...)
  - External call: ILeverageStrategiesManager(LEVERAGE_MANAGER).enterLeverage(...)
  - Event emitted before the call: EnterLeverageForwarded(...)
- contracts/RelativePositionManager/IRelativePositionManager.sol
  - function executePositionAccountCall(address positionAccount, address[] targets, bytes[] data) external;
  - Description indicates implementation calls: IPositionAccount(positionAccount).genericCalls(targets, data) without validating code/type/authorization.
- contracts/RelativePositionManager/RelativePositionManager.sol
  - function setPositionAccountImplementation(address positionAccountImpl)
  - Checks only: positionAccountImpl != address(0)
  - Locks permanently: isPositionAccountImplementationLocked = true; POSITION_ACCOUNT_IMPLEMENTATION = positionAccountImpl;
  - Event: PositionAccountImplementationSet(positionAccountImpl)
  - Risky downstream usage: ClonesUpgradeable.cloneDeterministic with an implementation that may have no code.

Recommended mitigations (combined from findings)
- Add code-size checks before external calls / configuration:
  - require(LEVERAGE_MANAGER.code.length > 0)
  - require(positionAccount.code.length > 0)
  - require(positionAccountImpl.code.length > 0) before locking POSITION_ACCOUNT_IMPLEMENTATION (ideally revert with a dedicated error).
- Prefer low-level call wrappers that enforce “contract target” behavior:
  - Use AddressUpgradeable.functionCall with abi.encodeCall(...) for forwarding to LEVERAGE_MANAGER so the transaction reverts if the target is not a contract.
- Validate that a provided positionAccount is the expected PositionAccount bound to this manager before executing genericCalls:
  - Check IPositionAccount(positionAccount).RELATIVE_POSITION_MANAGER() == address(this)
  - Optionally validate expected owner/longVToken/shortVToken for the position.

final_verdict.reason: Matches rule 6 (assumes_owner_mistake): the described “exploits” primarily require a privileged operator/admin to misconfigure critical addresses (e.g., setting LEVERAGE_MANAGER or POSITION_ACCOUNT_IMPLEMENTATION to an EOA/no-code address) or to supply an incorrect positionAccount address. The resulting silent no-op/bricking scenarios stem from providing wrong configuration/inputs rather than a demonstrated attacker-driven capability to force those values on-chain. Therefore it fits the invalid pattern of relying on owner/operator mistake.

Matches rule 6 (assumes_owner_mistake): the described “exploits” primarily require a privileged operator/admin to misconfigure critical addresses (e.g., setting LEVERAGE_MANAGER or POSITION_ACCOUNT_IMPLEMENTATION to an EOA/no-code address) or to supply an incorrect positionAccount address. The resulting silent no-op/bricking scenarios stem from providing wrong configuration/inputs rather than a demonstrated attacker-driven capability to force those values on-chain. Therefore it fits the invalid pattern of relying on owner/operator mistake.

invalid_rules_check.prompt:
You are the best security expert in web3 security auditing. 
    Your task is to check whether a given security finding matches any known invalid finding patterns and rules.
    
    **Instructions and Requirements**:
    1. Carefully analyze the finding and compare it against every single rule in the provided InvalidFindingRules.
    2. If a match is found, provide a concise explanation of why the finding is invalid based on the matched rule and set the verdict field to be "invalid".
    3. If no match is found, set the verdict field to "n/a" and set the reason field to "No invalid rules matched, further investigation needed".
    4. You can terminate the analysis early if you find a rule that matches the finding and hence marked the finding as invalid.
    5. Be strict: vague or speculative matches should be excluded. That is, only mark the finding as invalid if it clearly and directly falls under the rule's description. 
    6. You must check against each rule in the InvalidFindingRules list one by one, until a match is found or all rules have been checked.

    ** Output **
    Your output should be an instance of the AIRawFindingVerificationV2 class, which contains the following fields:
    - verdict:  A string indicating the verdict of the finding. At this stage, you can only set it to "invalid" if the finding matches any of the InvalidFindingRules or "n/a" if the finding does not match any of the InvalidFindingRules.
    - confidence: A string indicating the confidence level of the verdict. It can be "Strong", "Medium", or "Weak".
    - reason: A string explaining the reason for the verdict. It should be concise and clear in less than 5 sentences.
    - model: Do not set this field. Leave it empty.

    ** Input **
    Input 1. A **finding** in JSON format to be checked as delimited by <finding> tag.
    <finding>
    {'title': 'External call to LEVERAGE_MANAGER can silently succeed when the address has no code', 'description': 'Root cause & mechanism
Multiple parts of the RelativePositionManager system perform high-level external calls to addresses that are not verified to contain contract runtime code and/or to be the expected contract type. In the EVM, calling an address with no code (EOA, not-yet-deployed predeploy, or a self-destructed contract) can return success with empty returndata. When the Solidity call is to a function that returns no value (or when the caller does not decode/validate returndata), the call may not revert, so the caller can proceed (and even emit events) despite the callee having executed no logic. Similarly, calling a “wrong” contract that has a permissive fallback can also return success without performing the intended operation.

This creates “silent success” / “no-op” scenarios that can brick or desynchronize the position lifecycle and any off-chain/accounting logic that assumes the operation happened because the transaction succeeded and events were emitted.

Trigger conditions / assumptions
- A target address used for an external call is an EOA or otherwise has no runtime code (e.g., destroyed contract), or is not the expected contract (e.g., a non-PositionAccount contract with a permissive fallback).
- Specific triggers in this codebase include:
  1) LEVERAGE_MANAGER is configured to an address with no code; PositionAccount forwards enterLeverage to it.
  2) RelativePositionManager.executePositionAccountCall is invoked with a positionAccount address that is not a deployed PositionAccount tied to this manager (EOA, wrong contract, or contract with permissive fallback).
  3) RelativePositionManager.setPositionAccountImplementation is called with an implementation address that is non-zero but has no code; the value is then permanently locked, and clones can still be created pointing to a non-contract implementation.

Impact
- Silent no-op execution: functions can “succeed” on-chain while performing no leverage operation and/or no intended generic calls.
- Unexpected state and operational desynchronization: downstream logic (manager/UI/off-chain monitoring) may assume a position was opened/scaled or an emergency action executed because the transaction succeeded and relevant events were emitted (e.g., EnterLeverageForwarded), when nothing actually happened.
- Broken emergency procedures: executePositionAccountCall may report success while not rescuing/moving funds as expected.
- Bricked/incorrect position lifecycle due to misconfiguration: if POSITION_ACCOUNT_IMPLEMENTATION is set to an EOA/no-code address, ClonesUpgradeable.cloneDeterministic can still deploy minimal proxies referencing that implementation, and subsequent IPositionAccount interactions may not reliably revert (delegatecall to no-code can succeed with empty returndata). Because isPositionAccountImplementationLocked is set to true in the same transaction, this misconfiguration becomes effectively irreversible without upgrading the manager.

Exploitation / reproduction notes (PoC highlights)
- Configure LEVERAGE_MANAGER to an address with no runtime code, then call PositionAccount.enterLeverage(...). The call to ILeverageStrategiesManager(LEVERAGE_MANAGER).enterLeverage(...) can return success with empty returndata; enterLeverage will emit EnterLeverageForwarded and complete, despite no leverage strategy being executed.
- Call RelativePositionManager.executePositionAccountCall with positionAccount set to an EOA (or a contract with a permissive fallback). The high-level call IPositionAccount(positionAccount).genericCalls(targets, data) can return success without executing the intended logic, causing the manager-side operation to succeed silently.
- Call setPositionAccountImplementation with a non-zero EOA/no-code address. The contract locks the implementation (isPositionAccountImplementationLocked = true) and emits PositionAccountImplementationSet. Later clones created via ClonesUpgradeable.cloneDeterministic will point to the no-code implementation, leading to silent no-op / bricked behavior on subsequent interactions.

Code-location anchors (if present in the inputs)
- contracts/RelativePositionManager/PositionAccount.sol
  - function enterLeverage(...)
  - External call: ILeverageStrategiesManager(LEVERAGE_MANAGER).enterLeverage(...)
  - Event emitted before the call: EnterLeverageForwarded(...)
- contracts/RelativePositionManager/IRelativePositionManager.sol
  - function executePositionAccountCall(address positionAccount, address[] targets, bytes[] data) external;
  - Description indicates implementation calls: IPositionAccount(positionAccount).genericCalls(targets, data) without validating code/type/authorization.
- contracts/RelativePositionManager/RelativePositionManager.sol
  - function setPositionAccountImplementation(address positionAccountImpl)
  - Checks only: positionAccountImpl != address(0)
  - Locks permanently: isPositionAccountImplementationLocked = true; POSITION_ACCOUNT_IMPLEMENTATION = positionAccountImpl;
  - Event: PositionAccountImplementationSet(positionAccountImpl)
  - Risky downstream usage: ClonesUpgradeable.cloneDeterministic with an implementation that may have no code.

Recommended mitigations (combined from findings)
- Add code-size checks before external calls / configuration:
  - require(LEVERAGE_MANAGER.code.length > 0)
  - require(positionAccount.code.length > 0)
  - require(positionAccountImpl.code.length > 0) before locking POSITION_ACCOUNT_IMPLEMENTATION (ideally revert with a dedicated error).
- Prefer low-level call wrappers that enforce “contract target” behavior:
  - Use AddressUpgradeable.functionCall with abi.encodeCall(...) for forwarding to LEVERAGE_MANAGER so the transaction reverts if the target is not a contract.
- Validate that a provided positionAccount is the expected PositionAccount bound to this manager before executing genericCalls:
  - Check IPositionAccount(positionAccount).RELATIVE_POSITION_MANAGER() == address(this)
  - Optionally validate expected owner/longVToken/shortVToken for the position.'}
    </finding>

    Input 2. A list of **InvalidFindingRules** in JSON format as delimited by <InvalidFindingRules> tag. 
    <InvalidFindingRules>
    [{"id": 1, "name": "no_exploitable_issue", "description": "If the finding indicates that no exploitable issue were found, it should be marked as invalid.", "example": "No vulnerabilities found in the contract."}, {"id": 2, "name": "privileged_roles", "description": "Mark the finding as invalid if it requires the attacker to already possess privileged permissions (owner/admin/minter/manager/signer, etc.) AND the reported impact is fully achievable by that role through intended/explicitly authorized functionality (i.e., it is not a bypass, invariant break, or escalation beyond the documented role powers). If the finding shows that a privileged role can exceed its intended authority, bypass multisig/threshold, break safety invariants, steal assets contrary to policy, or permanently lock the system without an intended recovery path, then this rule does NOT apply.", "example": "invalid example: Owner can withdraw funds from the contract, and withdrawals are explicitly permitted by the contract design.
 n/a example: A single signatory can update metadata even though other administrative actions are protected by a multisig threshold, indicating a bypass of the intended authorization model."}, {"id": 3, "name": "first_caller_wins", "description": "If the finding describes a 'first-caller wins' scenario in functions related to initialization or similar (such as `initialize` function in many Solidity projects), it should be marked as invalid.", "example": "The first caller of the initialize function can set arbitrary values."}, {"id": 4, "name": "fee_on_transfer", "description": "If the finding is related to fee-on-transfer behaviour—i.e., transfer or transferFrom deducts a fee but still emits a single Transfer event with the original value—treat this discrepancy between events and state as non-critical and mark the finding as invalid.", "example": "Transfer event emitted with original value despite fee deduction."}, {"id": 5, "name": "requires_external_manipulation", "description": "If the exploit assumes the attacker can trick, persuade, or manipulate a privileged user to call a function, it should be marked as invalid. The exploit must be feasible without external influence.", "example": "Assuming a user is tricked into calling a transfer function with malicious input."}, {"id": 6, "name": "assumes_owner_mistake", "description": "If the finding assumes the contract owner makes a mistake (e.g., sets wrong values, uses incorrect proxies), it should be marked as invalid. But if it involves a coding mistake such as unintialized variables or roles, this rule does not apply.", "example": "Owner might accidentally set an unsafe high interest rate."}, {"id": 7, "name": "impractical_overflow_underflow", "description": "If the finding involves overflow or underflow that depends on impractically large values (e.g., approaching uint256 max or e18), and is not exploitable in realistic usage, it should be marked as invalid.", "example": "Array index can overflow only if index > 2^255."}, {"id": 8, "name": "requires_privilege_escalation", "description": "If the finding assumes the attacker gains privileged permissions (such as onlyOwner, onlyMinter) through unspecified or unrealistic means, it should be marked as invalid.", "example": "Assuming an attacker can trick, persuade, or manipulate the legitimate owner or another address to call a function, it should be considered invalid."}, {"id": 9, "name": "unrealistic_high_cost_attack", "description": "If the finding assumes the attacker would spend excessive resources, time, or money for a minimal or temporary impact (e.g., short-term DoS), it should be marked as invalid. Attacks should be cost-effective and realistic.", "example": "Attacker must spend 1 BTC to congest the network and temporarily disrupt service."}, {"id": 10, "name": "pure_operator_error_no_missing_validation", "description": "Mark the finding as invalid only if the described issue requires an owner/user/operator to provide incorrect configuration or parameters AND the contract already enforces all relevant on-chain validation/invariants such that correct usage prevents the issue. If the contract lacks validation (e.g., does not verify interfaces, does not bound-check parameters, accepts non-canonical inputs, or fails open on external dependency failure), then the finding is coding-related and must NOT be marked invalid under this rule.", "example": "Invalid: documentation clearly requires a specific precondition and the contract enforces it, but the finding assumes the operator ignores it. Valid: the contract does not validate that a configured external contract exposes required views, so misconfiguration can cause incorrect behavior."}, {"id": 11, "name": "incomplete_finding_description", "description": "Mark the finding as invalid if the finding description is obviously truncated or incomplete (e.g., abruptly cut off mid-sentence, unfinished bullet/section, dangling punctuation like an opening quote/parenthesis/bracket without a close, etc.). If the description appears complete (even if unclear or potentially incorrect), then this rule does NOT apply and should be marked as N/A. ", "example": {"invalid": "The attacker can drain funds by calling withdraw and", "n_a": "The attacker can drain funds by exploiting a missing access control check on withdraw(), allowing unauthorized transfers."}}]
    </InvalidFindingRules>

result.reason: Matches rule 6 (assumes_owner_mistake): the described “exploits” primarily require a privileged operator/admin to misconfigure critical addresses (e.g., setting LEVERAGE_MANAGER or POSITION_ACCOUNT_IMPLEMENTATION to an EOA/no-code address) or to supply an incorrect positionAccount address. The resulting silent no-op/bricking scenarios stem from providing wrong configuration/inputs rather than a demonstrated attacker-driven capability to force those values on-chain. Therefore it fits the invalid pattern of relying on owner/operator mistake.
